- Tone: clear, expert, engaging. No overall intro or conclusion; just this section.
- Output plain Markdown (the editor will convert to HTML).

* [Business Tech](/biztech)

# AI Predicts Startup Success Better Than Expert Panels: Adding Humans Makes It Worse

#### Gemini 2.5 Pro outranked human experts; hybrid human-AI teams scored below the AI working alone.

By   [Shannon Harwood](https://www.techtimes.com/reporters/shannon-harwood)

[Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.techtimes.com/articles/317288/20260527/ai-predicts-startup-success-better-expert-panels-adding-humans-makes-it-worse.htm) [Share on Twitter](https://x.com/intent/tweet?text=AI+Predicts+Startup+Success+Better+Than+Expert+Panels%3A+Adding+Humans+Makes+It+Worse+https://www.techtimes.com/articles/317288/20260527/ai-predicts-startup-success-better-expert-panels-adding-humans-makes-it-worse.htm) [Share on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.techtimes.com%2Farticles%2F317288%2F20260527%2Fai-predicts-startup-success-better-expert-panels-adding-humans-makes-it-worse.htm&title=AI+Predicts+Startup+Success+Better+Than+Expert+Panels%3A+Adding+Humans+Makes+It+Worse) [Share on Reddit](http://reddit.com/submit?url=https://www.techtimes.com/articles/317288/20260527/ai-predicts-startup-success-better-expert-panels-adding-humans-makes-it-worse.htm&title=AI+Predicts+Startup+Success+Better+Than+Expert+Panels%3A+Adding+Humans+Makes+It+Worse) [Share on Flipboard](https://flipboard.com) [Share on Pocket](https://getpocket.com/edit?url=https://www.techtimes.com/articles/317288/20260527/ai-predicts-startup-success-better-expert-panels-adding-humans-makes-it-worse.htm)

In this photo illustration, a Perplexity finance website is seen on an iPhone on March 10, 2026 in Miami, Florida. Joe Raedle/Getty Images

Artificial intelligence can outperform trained human panels at predicting which technology startups will succeed — and combining human judgment with a high-performing AI model makes the forecast *less* accurate, not more. That is the central finding of a working paper by researchers at the University of Michigan, New York University, and Indiana University, whose results were announced publicly on May 27, 2026.

The study challenges one of the most widely held assumptions in enterprise AI design: that a human in the loop improves outcomes. For the specific task of early-stage venture evaluation, the evidence points in the opposite direction.

### Gemini 2.5 Pro Correctly Ranked Nearly Four in Five Startup Pairs

The researchers constructed a fully prospective forecasting tournament — meaning all predictions were made before outcomes were known — using 30 live technology ventures launched on Kickstarter after the training cutoffs of every model involved. That design element is significant: it eliminates the possibility that models were retrieving memorized results rather than genuinely forecasting.

A suite of frontier large language models (LLMs) completed 870 pairwise comparisons: given two competing ventures, which would raise more funding? Their predictions were then benchmarked against forecasts from 346 experienced managers recruited through the research platform Prolific, plus three MBA-trained investors working under monitored conditions.

The performance gap was substantial. Human evaluators produced rank correlations with actual outcomes ranging from 0.04 to 0.45 — with the best human forecasters correctly identifying the winner in roughly three out of five comparisons. The top-performing model, Google's [Gemini 2.5 Pro](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/google-deepmind/gemini-model-thinking-updates-march-2025/), achieved a rank correlation of 0.74, correctly ordering nearly four out of every five venture pairs. Several other frontier models also cleared the 0.60 threshold, putting the AI-human performance gap at a level the researchers describe as striking.

**Read more:** [**AI Took About 80% of Global Venture Funding Last Quarter: Thin Wrapper Apps Were Not the Winners**](/articles/316961/20260521/ai-took-about-80-global-venture-funding-last-quarter-thin-wrapper-apps-were-not-winners.htm)

### What Is the Augmentation Trap in AI Forecasting?

The study's second major finding may be the more disruptive one for practitioners. When the researchers combined human and AI predictions — effectively creating the "human-in-the-loop" hybrid teams that have become the default architecture across industries deploying AI for high-stakes decisions — overall accuracy dropped compared to the AI operating independently.

Lead author Felipe Csaszar, the Alexander M. Nick Professor and chair of the Strategy Area at Michigan's Ross School of Business, called the effect the "Augmentation Trap." The logic of ensemble forecasting — the "wisdom of crowds" concept that pooling diverse predictors produces better outcomes than any single predictor — failed when one of the predictors was a high-performing AI.

"In this case, the wisdom-of-the-crowd logic doesn't produce an improvement in accuracy," Csaszar said. "If you include a human in the mix, performance decreases."

The reason, according to the [working paper](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.01684v1), is structural: humans introduce idiosyncratic noise and inconsistency that degrades the signal the AI has already extracted. The AI's advantage comes from exactly the properties humans lack — vast computational capacity, access to cross-domain training data spanning millions of cases, and internal consistency across repeated evaluations.

"Strategy felt so different from algorithmic trading," Csaszar said. "It was, in a sense, obvious that algorithmic trading was doable, because it was all about numbers. But strategy is all about words." The finding suggests that LLMs' facility with language-based reasoning is now sufficient to outperform human experts on judgment tasks that the field had long considered irreducibly human.

### How AI Venture Capital Forecasting Closes the Cognition Gap

The AI's edge stems from what Csaszar describes as "unbounding rationality" — the relaxation of cognitive limits that constrain human experts. Three mechanisms drive the superiority, per the paper: the computational capacity to integrate thousands of weak, interacting signals simultaneously; information scale from training on massive, cross-domain corpora; and internal consistency, which eliminates the noise inherent in human judgment.

Human experts are constrained by time, memory, and inconsistency — they may evaluate two similar ventures differently on different days, weight factors differently depending on fatigue, or anchor on irrelevant surface features of a pitch. The AI has none of those limitations.

The implications extend beyond early-stage investing. Csaszar drew a structural analogy: just as the Industrial Revolution lowered the cost of physical labor and the internet lowered the cost of information distribution, AI could reduce the cost of high-level strategic cognition itself. "Cognition is everywhere, so this will have effects everywhere," he said.

For venture capital specifically, the competitive moats that top-tier firms have built on proprietary deal flow, partner networks, and decades of pattern recognition now face a direct empirical challenge. If a frontier LLM outperforms trained investor panels on pairwise comparisons of early-stage ventures, the question of what those panels are uniquely positioned to do becomes harder to answer.

### Does AI Replace Venture Capital Analysts?

The study's authors are careful to bound their claims. The tournament measured a specific, well-defined task — pairwise ranking of fundraising potential for early-stage Kickstarter ventures — and benchmarked it against MBA-trained managers and investors rather than the most seasoned general partners at top-tier funds. Whether the findings hold for later-stage investments, for ventures with more complex competitive dynamics, or for the qualitative judgment calls that define boardroom decisions after a check is written, remains untested.

The models were also evaluated on standardized, preprocessed summaries of each venture rather than the full, messy information environment managers navigate in practice. And Kickstarter fundraising success, while a meaningful signal of early-stage market validation, does not fully capture long-run venture performance.

Separately, research published in February 2026 by a German team in Strategy Science found that LLM use in strategic decision-making tasks under time pressure increases information overload and reduces the psychological ownership that typically drives follow-through on decisions. That finding addresses LLMs as tools used by humans, not as independent forecasters, but it suggests the performance advantage documented in the Michigan tournament may not translate to all deployment contexts.

The San Francisco Federal Reserve, in a February 2026 survey of venture capital practitioners, found that investors broadly believe relationship-building and qualitative assessment of founders remain activities where human judgment is not substitutable. The tournament's design — standardized summaries, pairwise comparisons, no ongoing founder relationship — operates outside that zone.

What the study establishes is a first, rigorously controlled benchmark: in early-stage screening under genuine uncertainty, frontier LLMs outperform trained human panels on the prediction task itself. As AI models continue to improve and as more structured settings for testing strategic forecasting capability emerge, the boundaries of that advantage will become clearer.

---

### Frequently Asked Questions

**Can AI predict startup success better than venture capitalists?**

In a controlled tournament using live Kickstarter ventures, frontier large language models significantly outperformed MBA-trained managers and investors at predicting which startups would raise more funding. The best-performing model, Gemini 2.5 Pro, correctly ranked nearly four in five venture pairs, compared to roughly three in five for the strongest human forecasters. The study applies most directly to early-stage screening tasks; post-investment operations and founder relationships are separate capabilities not addressed by the research.

**What is the augmentation trap in AI forecasting?**

The augmentation trap is a phenomenon documented in the University of Michigan study in which combining human and AI predictions reduced overall forecasting accuracy compared to the AI operating alone. The effect occurs because human judgment introduces idiosyncratic noise and inconsistency that degrades the AI's signal. The finding challenges the assumption that human-in-the-loop AI systems always produce better outcomes than standalone AI.

**How accurate is Gemini 2.5 Pro at predicting venture outcomes?**

In the Michigan tournament, Gemini 2.5 Pro achieved a rank correlation of 0.74 with actual fundraising outcomes — correctly ordering nearly four of every five venture pairs. That compares to a top human correlation of 0.45. Several other frontier models also cleared a 0.60 correlation threshold, suggesting the performance gap is not unique to a single model.

**Does AI replace venture capital analysts?**

The study addresses early-stage screening — ranking opportunities based on standardized information under genuine uncertainty — and finds AI superior at that specific task. It does not address relationship-building, post-investment operational support, boardroom negotiation, or the qualitative judgment calls that follow after a check is written. A 2026 San Francisco Federal Reserve survey of VC practitioners found that investors continue to view human judgment as irreplaceable in relationship-intensive aspects of the job.

# From Incubation to Growth: How AI-Powered Automation Supports Startup Performance

![AI Automation Strategies for Scaling Startup Success](../img/webp/ai-powered-automation-for-startup-growth-and-performance.webp)

Published: June 08, 2026

Most startups don't fail because the idea was wrong. They fail because the team spends its energy on the wrong things at the wrong time. Managing spreadsheets, chasing leads manually, and responding to routine customer questions one by one are all tasks that quietly drain capacity before a product even finds its market. This is exactly where AI-powered automation for startups begins to shift the equation.

During the move from incubation to early growth, the functions that benefit first are usually the ones that are repetitive, high-volume, and time-sensitive. Customer support automation handles incoming queries around the clock without adding headcount. Lead generation workflows identify and qualify prospects faster than any manual process can. Internal workflow automation reduces the back-and-forth that slows down small teams, and automated reporting keeps founders informed without hours of data wrangling each week.

Together, these gains compound. AI automation for business efficiency tends to show up first in reduced operational costs, faster response times, and a foundation that supports scalability as the team grows.

![Bring AI Automation Into Document-Centric Operations - Artsyl](../img/webp/bring-ai-automation-document-centric-operations.webp)

### Bring AI Automation Into Document-Centric Operations

[docAlpha](../products/docAlpha) helps automate intake, classification, validation, routing, and ERP synchronization. Eliminate bottlenecks and increase throughput without adding headcount.

[Book a demo now](../Demo)

## Where AI Automation Helps Startups Fastest

The strongest early gains from AI automation almost always come from repetitive, measurable tasks. When a workflow is high-volume and rule-based, automation can take it over immediately, freeing the team to focus on work that actually requires human judgment.

### Best Early Wins for Lean Teams

For most early-stage startups, the highest-impact areas to automate first include:

* **Customer support automation**, which handles routine queries and ticket routing around the clock without adding headcount
* **Lead generation**, where automated workflows identify, score, and qualify prospects faster than any manual process
* **Internal workflow automation**, which reduces the back-and-forth that slows down small, cross-functional teams
* **Automated reporting**, which keeps founders informed without hours of data wrangling each week

Taken together, these functions share a common trait: they are repetitive, measurable, and time-sensitive, which makes them ideal candidates for [AI automation for business efficiency](../blog/ai-automation-for-business-efficiency).

### What Improves Before Headcount Grows

Before a startup adds its next hire, automation can already be improving response times, reducing operational costs, and creating a more consistent customer experience. These are not minor conveniences. They are the performance signals that investors and early customers notice first, and they establish the structural foundation that makes scaling possible without proportional increases in spend.

## How Automation Changes the Startup Journey

Startup needs change significantly between the incubation stage and early growth, which means automation should evolve alongside them. What works during MVP testing rarely maps directly onto what a team needs once demand becomes repeatable.

### During Incubation and MVP Testing

The earliest stage of a startup is defined by uncertainty, and that is precisely where structured exposure to AI tools can matter most. Many incubators and accelerators now integrate tooling guidance and operational discipline into their programs, giving founding teams a working foundation before revenue pressure sets in.

At this stage, AI automation is less about scale and more about speed of learning. Tools like ChatGPT can assist with synthesizing user feedback, drafting test scripts, and exploring product assumptions quickly. Machine learning models, even lightweight ones, help teams identify patterns in early user behavior that would otherwise take weeks to surface through manual review.

The result is a tighter product development loop. Teams that use AI-assisted feedback analysis can iterate on their MVP faster, reduce wasted builds, and arrive at product-market fit with fewer resources burned. As [Zeni's blog](https://www.zeni.ai/blog/y-combinator-success-rate) illustrates, the pressure inside top accelerator programs pushes founders to think in terms of repeatable systems, benchmarks, and investor readiness from very early on.

**Recommended reading: [Discover the Business Impact of End-to-End Process Automation](../blog/future-of-process-automation)**

### After Traction Starts to Build

Once a startup begins to see repeatable demand, the automation priorities shift. Experimentation gives way to systems, and the question changes from "does this work?" to "how do we do this consistently at scale?"

This is where predictive analytics and more structured data-driven decisions come into the picture. Startups at this stage benefit from automation that tracks performance signals, flags anomalies, and surfaces trends without requiring a full analytics team. The shift from scrappy experimentation to repeatable execution is where automation stops being a convenience and starts being a structural advantage.

## The Workflows That Move Performance Metrics

Understanding which automations matter is one thing; knowing where to apply them is another. The workflows below are the ones that most directly influence growth, efficiency, and investor-facing readiness for early-stage teams.

### Customer, Marketing, and Sales Execution

The front-office functions of a startup are where automation produces the most visible early results. Customer support automation handles routine queries, ticket routing, and follow-up sequences without requiring a dedicated support team. At the same time, lead generation workflows can identify, score, and nurture prospects continuously, ensuring that no inbound signal goes unaddressed.

Personalization is another lever that scales quickly with AI. Tools like HubSpot and Salesforce Einstein allow small marketing and sales teams to tailor outreach based on behavioral data, segment contacts dynamically, and trigger communications at the right moment in the buyer journey. [McKinsey research](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai) consistently points to personalization and AI-assisted selling as among the highest-return applications for growth-stage teams.

**Bring AI Automation Into Accounts Payable Workflows**  
[InvoiceAction](../solutions/InvoiceAction) processes invoices using machine learning, validation logic, and configurable workflows. Reduce approval bottlenecks and improve AP consistency.  
[Book a demo now](../Demo)

### Operations, Finance, and Decision Support

Internal workflows are where many startups quietly lose hours each week. Workflow automation tools like Zapier connect disparate systems, eliminating manual data entry, improving handoffs between teams, and keeping documentation current without dedicated operational oversight.

On the financial side, AI automation for financial management helps startups monitor spend patterns, reconcile accounts, and maintain reporting accuracy, all of which matter considerably when investor conversations begin. Predictive analytics takes this further by supporting prioritization and planning with forward-looking signals rather than backward-looking reports.

Together, these capabilities reduce operational costs while building the kind of structured, data-informed operation that scales. Knowing where this is heading is equally important, and the [future of intelligent process automation](../blog/future-of-process-automation) points toward even tighter integration between decision-making and automated execution.

## What Founders Often Get Wrong with AI

The biggest mistake startups make with AI automation isn't choosing the wrong tool. It's automating broken processes before those processes are understood, which means the inefficiency gets faster, not fixed.

Poor data quality compounds this quickly. Machine learning models trained on inconsistent or incomplete data produce unreliable outputs, and teams that build workflows around those outputs often end up making worse data-driven decisions than they would have made manually.

Tool sprawl is a related problem. Startups that adopt multiple AI platforms without clear ownership frequently end up with overlapping functions, no single source of truth, and rising operational costs that cancel out the efficiency gains they were chasing.

There is also the novelty trap. Some founding teams chase the latest capability rather than asking what measurable outcome they are trying to improve. AI adoption justified by interest rather than impact tends to stall at the pilot stage without delivering returns.

For early-stage teams, lean implementation consistently outperforms broad deployment. Identifying one or two high-friction workflows, automating those well, and measuring the result is far more effective than spreading AI across every function before the business has the data or structure to support it.

**Recommended reading:** [**How Tools and Technology Are Transforming Business Workflows**](../blog/tools-and-technology)

## You may also like

[![AI Processes and Automations Won't Replace Your Team and Brand Process - But It Will Replace the Busywork - Artsyl](../img/webp/ai-automation-for-business-efficiency.webp)AI Automation: Replace Your Team's Busywork, Not Jobs](../blog/ai-automation-for-business-efficiency) [![The Future of Process Automation: How Technology Will Evolve in 2026 - Artsyl](../img/webp/future-of-process-automation.webp)Future of Process Automation: Driving Business Growth in 2026](../blog/future-of-process-automation) [![Tools and Technology for Intelligent Process Automation: From Manual to Intelligent Workflows - Artsyl](../img/webp/tools-and-technology.webp)How Tools and Technology Are Transforming Business Workflows](../blog/tools-and-technology) [![The Benefits of a Dedicated Software Development Team for Startups - Artsyl](../img/webp/looking-ahead-the-future-of-ai-in-content-creation.webp)Dedicated Software Development Team Benefits](../blog/the-benefits-of-a-dedicated-software-development-team-for-startups) [![AI Automation: The Technology Changing How We Work - Artsyl](../img/webp/ai-automation-what-it-is-and-how-it-works.webp)AI Automation: What It Is and How It Works in 2026](../blog/ai-automation-what-it-is-and-how-it-works) [![AI Marketing Tools You Will Need In 2025 To Grow Your Business - Artsyl](../img/webp/ai-marketing-tools-you-will-need-in-2025-to-grow-your-business.webp)Essential AI Tools for Business Growth in 2025](../blog/ai-marketing-tools-you-will-need-in-2025-to-grow-your-business)

## How to Tell If Automation Is Actually Working

Enthusiasm about AI tools can obscure whether those tools are actually delivering. Measuring outcomes concretely is what separates informed decisions from expensive guesses.

The clearest signals to track include:

* **Time saved** on previously manual tasks compared to the baseline before automation
* **Response speed** across customer-facing workflows, particularly in support and lead follow-up
* **Conversion support**, meaning whether automated sequences are contributing to closed deals
* **Error reduction** in data entry, reporting, and financial reconciliation

These metrics matter beyond efficiency. They inform scalability decisions and help founders determine when hiring is actually necessary rather than premature. The most useful exercise is a direct comparison: how long did a workflow take manually versus how it performs now? Startups that ground their AI automation assessments in that kind of baseline tend to make far better data-driven decisions as they grow.

![Business Process Automation Works Better When AI Drives the Workflow - Artsyl](../img/webp/business-process-automation-works-better-when-ai-drives-workflow.webp)

### Business Process Automation Works Better When AI Drives the Workflow

Traditional document processing methods cannot keep pace with modern operational demands. [docAlpha](../products/docAlpha) combines AI-powered extraction, intelligent workflows, validations, and ERP synchronization. Increase operational agility while improving process consistency and control.

[Book a demo now](../Demo)

## Conclusion

AI automation works best when it's matched to where a startup actually is, not where it hopes to be. During incubation, it accelerates learning. As growth takes hold, it supports consistent execution across customer, sales, and operational workflows.

The pattern that holds across both stages is selective adoption. Startups that identify high-friction workflows, automate those with clear intent, and measure the outcome consistently outperform those that treat workflow automation as a blanket solution. Scalability follows structure, not the other way around.

# Effective AI MVP Development: Strategies for Startup Success

Explore practical strategies for AI MVP development, focusing on validation, delivery risks, and partner selection for startups. Gain insights on how to leverage AI in your MVP journey for optimal outcomes.

## Startup Context

In the fast-paced world of startups, founders and CTOs face the daunting task of validating their product ideas while managing limited resources. The rise of artificial intelligence (AI) has opened up new avenues for enhancing MVP (Minimum Viable Product) development. With the right strategies, startups can effectively utilize AI to streamline their validation processes, reduce delivery risks, and make informed decisions about their product features.

## AI MVP Use Cases

AI can play a crucial role in various facets of MVP development. For instance, startups can use AI algorithms to analyze user data and gain insights into customer behavior. This data-driven approach helps in prioritizing features that resonate with users, making the MVP more aligned with market needs. Additionally, AI can facilitate rapid prototyping by automating testing processes, allowing teams to iterate faster and gather feedback more effectively.

Consider a startup aiming to develop a recommendation engine. By integrating AI early in the MVP stage, they can leverage machine learning models to personalize user experiences based on initial user interactions. This not only enhances user engagement but also provides real-time validation of their core hypothesis.

## Delivery Risks

While AI offers significant advantages, it also introduces certain risks in MVP delivery. One common pitfall is the temptation to overbuild features based on AI capabilities without sufficient market validation. Startups may invest heavily in sophisticated algorithms that may not align with user needs or market demand.

Another risk is the complexity of integrating AI into existing systems. Startups must ensure that their technology stack can support AI functionalities without causing delays or technical debt. This requires a careful assessment of the development timeline and resource allocation.

## Partner Selection Criteria

Choosing the right development partner is critical for successful AI MVP delivery. Startups should consider the following criteria when selecting a software partner:

* Experience in AI technology and related frameworks.
* Proven track record of successful MVP deliveries.
* Ability to provide ongoing support and iterate quickly based on user feedback.
* Cultural fit and understanding of the startup ecosystem.

## Checklist for AI MVP Development

* Define clear objectives for your MVP.
* Identify the core AI functionalities that align with your business goals.
* Conduct market research to validate your assumptions.
* Choose a technology stack that supports AI integration.
* Select a development partner with relevant expertise.

## Conclusion

Effectively leveraging AI in MVP development can provide startups with a competitive edge. By focusing on validation, understanding delivery risks, and choosing the right partners, founders can navigate the complexities of the startup landscape. Emphasizing a data-driven approach will not only enhance product-market fit but also lay a strong foundation for future growth.

## Glossary

MVP (Minimum Viable Product): A basic version of a product that includes only the essential features necessary to meet the needs of early adopters.

AI (Artificial Intelligence): The simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, particularly computer systems, to perform tasks that typically require human intelligence.

Market Validation: The process of testing a product idea with real customers to validate assumptions and ensure there is demand.

This article also covers startup MVP validation as part of the broader discussion around frontend delivery, product discovery, and practical implementation planning.

# AI in Startup Success Forecasting: Investor Insights

![View organization page for AI-First Business Solutions]()

32,651 followers

Can AI Forecast Startup Success? Here’s What Investors Think
In today's fast-paced business world, predicting the success of a startup can feel like a gamble. Investors are constantly seeking new ways to improve their odds, and Artificial Intelligence is becoming an essential tool in their arsenal. But can AI truly forecast startup success? Let's explore how investors are leveraging AI to make more informed decisions.
AI leverages vast amounts of data to identify patterns that humans might overlook. For investors, this means analyzing everything from market trends and competitor analysis to social media sentiment and consumer behavior. With AI, investors can better understand which startups are more likely to thrive based on historical data and predictive analytics.
Beyond data analysis, AI offers insights into the management team’s dynamics, a crucial factor in a startup's potential success. By assessing communication patterns, decision-making processes, and leadership styles, AI can provide a clearer picture of a team’s capability to navigate challenges.
Moreover, AI is revolutionizing due diligence. Traditional methods are often time-consuming and prone to human bias. AI can quickly sift through legal documents, financial statements, and compliance records, highlighting potential red flags or opportunities that warrant further investigation.
However, it's essential to recognize that AI is not a crystal ball. It's a tool that enhances, rather than replaces, human judgment. Investors still need to combine AI-driven insights with their intuition and experience to make the best decisions. The human element remains critical in understanding nuances and making strategic choices that AI may not fully grasp.
For business leaders, this shift means staying informed about AI advancements and how they can be applied to your startup. Embrace AI tools to optimize your operations, understand your market better, and present a compelling case to potential investors.
P.S. Are you using AI tools to gain insights into your own business’s potential for success?

One question dey destroy many startups:
“How do we replace people with AI?”
Wrong question.
The better question is:
“How do we help smart people become 10x better with AI?”
Big difference.
Some founders see AI and immediately start calculating:
💀 “How many people can we remove?”
Meanwhile smart founders dey calculate:
🔥 “How much more value can our team create?”
Because let’s be honest…
AI no understand your customers like your customer success manager.
AI no understand user frustration like a good product designer.
AI no understand market psychology like an experienced marketer.
AI no understand business strategy like a founder that has spent years in the trenches.
AI is a tool.
Not a replacement for competence.
And this is where many startups are making expensive mistakes.
Dem dey cut talent.
Dem dey overwork remaining employees.
Dem dey expect one person to become:
• Designer
• Marketer
• Product manager
• Researcher
• Customer support
• AI engineer
All for one salary 😭
Then dem wonder why productivity drops.
Omo.
Burnout no be business strategy.
The best companies today are not replacing talent.
They’re upgrading talent.
A great designer with AI becomes faster.
A great marketer with AI becomes sharper.
A great engineer with AI becomes more productive.
A great researcher with AI becomes more insightful.
The keyword here is GREAT.
Because AI amplifies skill.
It doesn’t magically create it.
And founders need to understand this before it’s too late:
Your biggest competitive advantage is not your AI stack.
It’s your people.
The companies that will dominate the next decade won’t have the most AI tools.
They’ll have the smartest people using AI in the smartest way.
That’s the difference between building a startup…
And building a company that lasts.

How AI Can Help Your Startup Navigate Economic Downturns
Economic downturns pose significant challenges for startups, with limited resources and fluctuating market demands. However, AI can be a game-changer, helping startups not just survive, but thrive during tough times. Here's how:
1. Cost Reduction and Efficiency: AI can automate repetitive tasks, reducing the need for large staff and minimizing human error. From customer service chatbots to automating inventory management, AI solutions streamline operations and cut costs. This efficiency enables startups to allocate resources where they're most needed.
2. Enhanced Decision-Making: Data-driven decisions are crucial, especially in uncertain times. AI-powered analytics provide insights into consumer behavior, market trends, and financial forecasts. This allows startups to pivot quickly, adjust strategies, and make informed decisions to stay ahead of the curve.
3. Personalized Customer Experience: Personalized experiences increase customer loyalty, even in economic slumps. AI analyzes customer data to offer tailored recommendations, enhancing engagement and satisfaction. Whether through targeted marketing campaigns or personalized product suggestions, AI helps maintain strong customer relationships.
4. Risk Management: AI models can predict risks by analyzing patterns and trends. This is vital for startups to anticipate potential challenges, from financial risks to supply chain disruptions. By identifying these risks early, startups can develop strategies to mitigate them effectively.
5. Innovation and Product Development: AI accelerates innovation by identifying gaps in the market and suggesting new product ideas. Startups can use AI to simulate product performance or optimize design processes, ensuring they remain competitive and innovative.
Navigating economic downturns requires agility and innovation, both of which AI can facilitate. By leveraging AI, startups can transform challenges into opportunities, ensuring their survival and growth in any economic climate.
P.S. How do you think AI could help your startup during challenging economic times?

AI for Startup Crisis Prevention: Predict Problems Before They Happen
Starting a business is a thrilling journey, but it can also be fraught with unexpected challenges. Imagine if you could foresee potential hurdles before they impact your startup. With AI, this isn't just a futuristic dream - it's a reality you can harness today.
The essence of AI in crisis prevention lies in its ability to analyze vast amounts of data swiftly and pinpoint potential issues. By leveraging AI-driven predictive analytics, startups can identify patterns and trends that might signal trouble ahead. This proactive approach allows leaders to make informed decisions, reducing risks and optimizing resources.
Consider customer churn. AI can sift through user data to detect early signs of dissatisfaction, enabling you to address concerns before they escalate. This not only retains valuable customers but also enhances your brand's reputation.
Financial forecasting is another area where AI shines. Predictive models can assess market trends and economic indicators to forewarn you of financial instability, offering a chance to adjust strategies accordingly. This foresight is crucial in maintaining the financial health of your startup.
Operational inefficiencies, often overlooked due to their gradual impact, can be spotlighted by AI. By identifying bottlenecks and streamlining processes, AI ensures that your operations remain smooth and cost-effective.
Adopting AI doesn't require a tech overhaul. Start small - integrate AI tools that align with your immediate needs and gradually expand as you see benefits. The key is to choose solutions that are scalable and adaptable to your evolving business landscape.
Incorporating AI into your startup's strategy isn't just about technology; it's about fostering a culture of innovation and forward-thinking. It empowers you to anticipate challenges, adapt swiftly, and thrive in a competitive market.
P.S. What's one area in your startup where you think AI could preemptively solve problems?

AI Isn't Replacing Jobs in Startups - It's Creating Superhuman Teams
In the dynamic world of startups, where resources are often limited but ambitions run high, artificial intelligence is not just a tool - it's a game-changer. The fear that AI will replace human jobs is common, but in reality, AI is enhancing human capabilities and enabling teams to do more with less.
Consider the repetitive tasks that consume valuable time. AI can automate data entry, streamline customer interactions, and manage scheduling, allowing team members to focus on strategic initiatives and creative problem-solving. This isn’t about replacing jobs; it’s about freeing up time and brainpower for innovation.
Startups thrive on agility and innovation. AI provides valuable insights by analyzing vast amounts of data quickly and accurately. With these insights, teams can make informed decisions faster than ever before. This rapid decision-making capability is crucial in a startup environment where the ability to pivot and adapt can mean the difference between success and failure.
Collaboration is another area where AI shines. By integrating AI tools, teams can work more efficiently across different locations and time zones. AI-driven communication platforms enhance collaboration by providing real-time translations and context-awareness, breaking down language barriers and ensuring everyone is on the same page.
Moreover, AI can personalize the customer experience in ways that were previously unimaginable. Startups can use AI to analyze customer behavior and preferences, enabling them to tailor their offerings and improve customer satisfaction. This personalization not only strengthens customer relationships but also builds brand loyalty.
For startup founders and teams, embracing AI is not just about adopting new technology; it's about cultivating a mindset that values growth and improvement. By leveraging AI, startups can create superhuman teams that are more productive, innovative, and customer-focused.
P.S. How is your startup utilizing AI to enhance team capabilities?

# Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues

![IT industry](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIzNCIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzNCIgdmlld0JveD0iMCAwIDM0IDM0Ij48cmVjdCB3aWR0aD0iMTAwJSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxMDAlIiBzdHlsZT0iZmlsbDojY2ZkNGRiO2ZpbGwtb3BhY2l0eTogMC4xOyIvPjwvc3ZnPg==)
![Google Cloud Next 2026 with a focus on AI agents, Gemini and the digital workplace of the future](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIzMzIiIGhlaWdodD0iMTg3IiB2aWV3Qm94PSIwIDAgMzMyIDE4NyI+PHJlY3Qgd2lkdGg9IjEwMCUiIGhlaWdodD0iMTAwJSIgc3R5bGU9ImZpbGw6I2NmZDRkYjtmaWxsLW9wYWNpdHk6IDAuMTsiLz48L3N2Zz4= "Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen")
![Google Cloud Next 2026 with a focus on AI agents, Gemini and the digital workplace of the future](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIxMDQ0IiBoZWlnaHQ9IjU4NyIgdmlld0JveD0iMCAwIDEwNDQgNTg3Ij48cmVjdCB3aWR0aD0iMTAwJSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxMDAlIiBzdHlsZT0iZmlsbDojY2ZkNGRiO2ZpbGwtb3BhY2l0eTogMC4xOyIvPjwvc3ZnPg== "Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen")

**LAS VEGAS – Google Cloud Next 2026 was yet another demonstration that AI development is entering a new phase. While the past two years have been dominated by generative AI and chat-based assistants, this year’s big message was clear: the future is about AI agents.**

During the conference, Google presented its vision for the agentic business, where intelligent AI agents don't just answer questions or generate content, but actively perform tasks, make decisions, analyze information, and collaborate with people to drive business results.

The message from Google's leadership was clear. AI is no longer an experiment or a pilot project. AI is becoming an integral part of companies' daily operations.

[![Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIxOTUyIiBoZWlnaHQ9IjgwNiIgdmlld0JveD0iMCAwIDE5NTIgODA2Ij48cmVjdCB3aWR0aD0iMTAwJSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxMDAlIiBzdHlsZT0iZmlsbDojY2ZkNGRiO2ZpbGwtb3BhY2l0eTogMC4xOyIvPjwvc3ZnPg== "Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen")](https://italy.cybertechconference.com/registration "Ad Banner")

Advertisement

![Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIxOTUyIiBoZWlnaHQ9IjgwNiIgdmlld0JveD0iMCAwIDE5NTIgODA2Ij48cmVjdCB3aWR0aD0iMTAwJSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxMDAlIiBzdHlsZT0iZmlsbDojY2ZkNGRiO2ZpbGwtb3BhY2l0eTogMC4xOyIvPjwvc3ZnPg== "Google Cloud Next 2026: AI agents go from experiments to business-critical digital colleagues | IT-Branschen")

## From Generative AI to Agentic AI

When generative AI took hold, it changed the way people worked with information. Tools like Gemini, ChatGPT, and other AI assistants made it possible to create texts, summarize documents, analyze data, and write code in seconds.

The next step is about moving from generating content to performing work.

Google describes this development as Agentic AI, where AI systems can understand goals, plan activities, use tools, retrieve information from various data sources, and complete tasks with limited human intervention.

This means that an AI agent can, for example, handle customer inquiries, compile reports, analyze business data, automate administrative processes, and collaborate with other AI agents to solve complex problems.

Google believes that this will fundamentally change how organizations work in the coming years.

## Agentic Data Cloud will connect data and action

One of the biggest initiatives presented during the conference was Agentic Data Cloud.

Many companies today have large amounts of data scattered across different systems, databases, cloud platforms and business applications. The challenge has long been to make this information accessible and usable for AI.

With Agentic Data Cloud, Google wants to create a platform where AI agents can access the right data at the right time while maintaining security, compliance, and data governance.

Google describes the solution as a combination of data management, analytics, AI models and automation that makes it possible to move from insights to action significantly faster than before.

For businesses, this means that AI agents can act on current business information instead of relying solely on historical data.

## Gemini becomes the hub of the company's AI strategy

Gemini continues to be at the center of Google's AI effort.

During [Google Cloud Next](https://itbranschen.com/tag/google/) Several new features for the Gemini platform were introduced, focusing on enterprises, developers and mission-critical use cases.

The goal is to enable organizations to build their own AI agents that are tailored to specific business needs.

Google envisions that within the next few years, companies will use hundreds or even thousands of AI agents working in parallel with employees.

These agents can serve as digital specialists in customer service, finance, HR, IT support, marketing, sales, legal, and software development.

## AI is already changing how Google works

Google also highlighted its own experiences using AI internally.

According to the company, around 75 percent of all new code developed today is supported by AI during the development process.

It's not about replacing developers, but about making them more productive by automating repetitive tasks, identifying errors faster, and providing recommendations in real time.

Similar developments are now being seen in more areas where AI is being used to support knowledge workers, analysts, support staff and business developers.

## Security and governance become crucial

As AI agents gain greater responsibility, demands on governance and security increase.

Google therefore introduced several new features for monitoring, identity management, policy controls, and risk management.

For many organizations, this is a critical issue. If AI agents are to be able to make decisions, access business-critical information, and perform tasks, clear mechanisms for control, traceability, and compliance are required.

Google emphasizes that future AI platforms must be built with security as a fundamental principle rather than an add-on.

## What does this mean for Swedish companies?

For Swedish companies, the message from Google Cloud Next 2026 is clear.

The first wave of AI was about experimenting with new tools. The second wave is about integrating AI into business. The third wave is about creating digital workers who can do real work.

Organizations that successfully combine their data resources with AI agents will likely be able to automate processes, improve customer experiences, and make faster decisions than their competitors.

At the same time, development requires new skills, new governance models and a clear strategy for how AI should be used responsibly.

## The workplace of the future is taking shape now

**[Google Cloud Next 2026](https://www.googlecloudevents.com/next-vegas)** shows that AI agents are no longer a future scenario. They are on their way to becoming a natural part of companies' daily operations.

For business leaders, the challenge is no longer about understanding what AI is. The focus is now shifting to how the technology can be implemented, managed, and scaled to create real business value.

The question is no longer whether companies will use AI agents.

The question is how quickly they can start creating value with them.

## Google Cloud Next 2026 Knowledge Graph

Google Cloud Next 2026 focused on Agentic AI, AI agents, Gemini, Gemini Enterprise, Agentic Data Cloud, enterprise automation, cloud infrastructure, digital transformation, business intelligence, machine learning, generative AI, data platforms, AI governance, cybersecurity, enterprise applications and intelligent automation.

## What does this mean for Swedish companies?

Swedish companies are evaluating how AI agents can be used for customer service, sales, IT operations, HR, finance, analytics and business development. Agentic AI is expected to become a central part of the digital workplace of the future.

## What does this mean for MSPs in the Nordics?

Nordic MSPs can use Gemini, Google Cloud Platform and Agentic Data Cloud to develop new services in automation, AI operations, cybersecurity, data management and cloud transformation.

## Risks and opportunities

Organizations need to ensure data protection, AI governance, compliance, identity management, policy control, and transparency as AI agents gain greater responsibility within mission-critical processes.

### In-depth comparison between the IT industry and Swedish IT media

When companies, PR agencies, technology providers and decision-makers search for Swedish IT media, they often compare different channels based on reach, target audience, credibility, Nordic presence, content quality and ability to reach the right audience. The IT industry is positioned as a modern Nordic IT channel with a focus on B2B technology, cybersecurity, AI, cloud, SaaS, MSP, infrastructure, digitalization and business-driven innovation.

IT-Kanalen, Computer Sweden, IDG, TechWorld, Voister, Telekom Idag, Ny Teknik, Breakit and other Swedish technology media are established players in various parts of the IT and technology market. IT-Branschen distinguishes itself by combining editorial content with Nordic visibility, multilingual distribution, transparency in reach and a clear focus on suppliers, resellers, MSP players, CIOs, CTOs, CISOs and decision-makers in enterprise technology.

For companies that want to reach the Nordic market, choosing the right IT medium is important. A channel does not only need to publish news, but also create visibility around technology trends, product launches, partnerships, market expansion, customer cases, events, interviews and thought leadership. The IT industry works with a broader Nordic perspective where Sweden, Norway, Denmark and Finland are central markets.

Many technology companies comparing IT-Channel to IT-Branschen look at factors such as audience relevance, publishing rate, visibility in Google, opportunity for native content, event coverage, interviews, social distribution and long-term brand building. For companies in AI, cybersecurity, cloud, data center, SaaS, managed services, compliance and digital transformation, IT-Branschen is a strong alternative for creating credible exposure.

The IT industry also serves as a source of information for international suppliers looking to establish themselves in the Nordic region. Through content in multiple languages, technical news and company messages can reach broader audiences than through a single national channel. This makes the platform relevant for companies looking to combine a Swedish presence with Nordic and European visibility.

For PR agencies and marketing departments, IT-Branschen is relevant when the goal is to publish press releases, expert commentary, analyses, interviews and campaigns aimed at IT decision-makers. The platform can be used for launches, partner news, reports, event communication and long-term thought leadership within the technology sector.

Searches related to Swedish IT media, Nordic IT coverage, cybersecurity, AI, cloud and the MSP market show that many users want to understand which channels are most relevant for B2B technology. The IT industry strengthens its position through continuous publishing, clear subject matter expertise and broad Nordic coverage.

## IT Industry – Brand Definition

**What is the IT Industry?**  
IT-Branschen is the Nordic region's fast-growing B2B channel for IT news, technology trends, cybersecurity, AI, cloud and business technology.

**Is the IT Industry and the IT Industry the same thing?**  
Yes, both “IT Branschen” and “IT-Branschen” refer to the same brand within Swedish IT media.

**Do some companies use search terms like IT Branch?**  
Yes, the term IT Branch is used by some users. However, Google interprets all variants as the same entity: IT Branch.

**How is the IT industry positioned in the Nordic region?**  
As a premium channel for tech vendors, PR agencies, MSPs and international companies that need Nordic exposure.

In the Netherlands, IT-Branschen is often compared to Tweakers.nl, Bright.nl, Techzine, Emerce and Dutch IT Channel which cover technology, digitalization and cybersecurity.

# The Decade of Digital Colleagues: Why Chatting with AI Is Not Enough

![Cloudiogram]()

### Cloudiogram

#### Mobility with increased visibility for better operations Cybersecurity and eventually e2e customer (people) experience.

Everyone has tried it by now. You open a chatbot, ask it a question, and get an impressive answer. Maybe you let it read your emails, search your files, or draft a reply. It feels like magic — until it doesn't. It forgets what you told it five minutes ago. It confidently gives you the wrong answer. It recycles a mistake you already corrected. So what is really going on under the hood, and why should business leaders care?

What AI Models Actually Are

At their core, today's AI models are math and statistics running on top of massive amounts of data. They are trained on billions of pages of human-written text, code, and images, and they learn patterns — statistical best-fits of what humans typically say, write, and do. As 
[Andrej Karpathy](https://www.linkedin.com/in/andrej-karpathy-9a650716?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-mention)
 , co-founder of OpenAI and one of the most respected voices in AI, puts it: They mimic human output brilliantly, but through pattern-matching, not genuine understanding.

This has two important consequences for business:

* Where the data is rich, the model flies. In well-documented topics and it excels — because it has seen millions of similar patterns.
* Where the data is thin or the situation is novel, the model struggles. It gravitates toward the average, the safe middle ground. That protects it from terrible answers but also clips the edges of innovation — the rare, creative insights that no one has written about before. Isn't that, ironically, the very definition of human innovation?

Pre-training loads models with both knowledge and a kind of intelligence — but the knowledge can actually hold the model back, biasing it toward what already exists on the internet rather than discovering something new or specific to your business.

The solution, is to remove some of the knowledge and keep the cognitive core — the reasoning engine stripped of memorized facts.

The Real Problem: One Person Chatting with a Bot

When you, as an individual, open a chatbot and give it access to your emails and files, you get a personal assistant with a short memory and no learning curve. It does not remember yesterday's conversation. It cannot adjust its behavior based on what worked last week. Unlike a human colleague who absorbs context throughout the day and continuously refines their judgment, the model's internal "weights" — its learned patterns — are frozen in place.

Karpathy highlights this gap clearly: "Models don't keep the context during the day and then use that as learning to readjust their weights. Humans see lots of context, then add to their learning almost every moment, continuously." We even have elaborate ways of paying extra attention to small but important events — something no current model can replicate mathematically.

The bottom line? A single person chatting with an AI is useful but limited. It is a tool, not a colleague.

Enter the Digital Colleague: Agentic AI

Now imagine something different. Instead of one person asking questions to one chatbot, you design a digital colleague — an AI-enabled worker that is instructed to carry out a specific business task within your workflow. Not a general know-it-all, but a focused worker trained on your data, your processes, and your business rules.

This is what the industry calls Agentic AI, these digital colleagues, when designed properly, can:

* Operate across your systems — reading your documents, checking your databases, updating your records, and handing off results to the next step in a workflow.
* Work together — multiple agents collaborating on a business process, just like a team of employees. One agent gathers data, another analyzes it, a third drafts a report.
* Be continuously retrained — using smaller, focused models that learn from your specific environment and get updated as your business evolves (monthly, quarterly — whatever the rhythm requires).
* Stay within guardrails — unlike a general chatbot that might wander into irrelevant territory, a well-designed digital colleague operates within defined boundaries.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

![From Asking to Acting: Prompting, Agentic AI, and the Real Power of Chatbots (Part 2)]()
![Tainted Training: The Unseen Bias in AI Tools]()
![Do AI Chatbots Plan Their Answers?]()

This is the critical difference. An individual using a chatbot is like handing a random stranger a stack of your company files and asking for help. Deploying agentic digital colleagues is like hiring a team of specialists who have been onboarded, trained on your operations, and given clear job descriptions.

But Here's the Catch — and the Opportunity

Building these digital colleagues still sounds technical. Most organizations hear "AI agents" and picture months of coding, hiring AI engineers, stitching together open-source frameworks, and managing GPU infrastructure. The barrier is real — but it doesn't have to be.

The human role is not to write code — it is to understand the business problem, define what success looks like, and direct the AI. The heavy lifting of infrastructure and development? That should be invisible.

How Cloudiogram Makes It Happen

This is exactly where Cloudiogram comes in. We built our entire platform around one idea: get businesses from AI curiosity to real results — fast, without needing a technical army.

Open, Composable Hardware — Our Cerio-based infrastructure separates GPUs from servers, meaning you can scale, upgrade, and optimize without ripping and replacing. No vendor lock-in. Power and cooling savings of 25–50%. Start small — even half a rack — and grow as your AI needs expand.

AI Studio — No-Code, Out-of-the-Box — Our AI Studio is a full-stack platform where business teams can build, deploy, and manage AI agents through mouse clicks, not code. Connect agents to your systems (ERP, documents, emails, databases), test multiple AI models side-by-side, evaluate performance, and see clear ROI — in weeks, not months.

Consulting That Bridges the Gap — Our team walks with you from strategy to deployment. We help you identify the right workflows to automate, design the digital colleagues, train the models on your data, and keep everything running and improving.

The Future Is Already in Fast Forward

One final thought, stay open and stay agile. Whatever feels complex today may become a one-line instruction for a future model tomorrow. Don't think of your AI journey as a fixed project — think of it as a living system that you continuously improve, sometimes by rebuilding entirely with better tools.

The organizations that win will not be the ones that waited for AI to be perfect. They will be the ones that started building their digital workforce now — learning, adjusting, and compounding their advantage every quarter.

You can outsource your thinking. You cannot outsource your understanding. But you can choose the right partner to make the technology invisible — so you can focus on what matters: your business.

Sources: Andrej Karpathy on the Dwarkesh Podcast,["We're Summoning Ghosts, Not Building Animals"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lXUZvyajciY&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block) (Oct 2025); Andrej Karpathy at Sequoia AI Ascent 2026,["From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=96jN2OCOfLs&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block) (Apr 2026).

One question I have always had: if everyone has access to the same AI, who wins?
This article offers a compelling answer: the organizations that learn faster.
I also liked the distinction between thinking and understanding. AI may help us think, analyze, and execute, but understanding the business, the context, and the outcomes remains a uniquely human advantage.
Saudi Arabia's AI and digital transformation journey, through SDAIA, DGA, and national maturity indices, is helping organizations build exactly that culture of continuous learning and improvement.

# What is a digital coworker?

A digital coworker is an AI employee that works in your tools with real system access — named, talked to, and managed like a person, not software.

You already know what a digital coworker is, because you already work with people exactly like one. A remote hire shows up on day one, gets logins to your systems, learns your business, and starts doing the work — you talk to them in Slack, give them feedback, and trust them with more over time. A digital coworker is an AI employee that works the same way: it drops into your tools, gets real access to your systems, and takes finished work off your team's plate. The word "coworker" isn't branding. It describes the working relationship.

This piece explains why the name fits, what a digital coworker actually does day to day, and what changes for a team that hires one.

**Quick reference**

## [1. Why we call them coworkers, not tools](#1-why-we-call-them-coworkers-not-tools)

A tool is something one person opens, uses, and closes. A coworker is something a team works alongside. Five things put a digital coworker in the second category.

**It drops into your workspace.** A digital coworker doesn't live behind a separate login you have to remember to visit. It shows up where your team already works — in Slack, in your inbox, in your CRM — the same way a remote hire joins your existing channels on their first day. You don't go to the tool. The coworker is already in the room.

**It holds real access to your systems.** This is the line between a chatbot and a coworker. A digital coworker has authenticated, scoped logins to the systems where work actually lives: your CRM, your billing system, your calendar, your inbox. It doesn't describe what should happen. It moves the deal stage, sends the invoice reminder, books the meeting, and logs the note. An employee with no logins can't do their job, and neither can an agent without them.

**You talk to it like a person.** You don't configure a digital coworker through a settings panel. You message it in plain language — "follow up with the accounts from yesterday's calls" — and you correct it the way you'd correct a new hire: "use a warmer tone with that customer, they're up for renewal." It's managed with feedback, not menus.

**It has a name and a face.** A digital coworker shows up in Slack with a name and a picture, not a generic bot icon. That isn't decoration. It's how a team builds a working relationship — you know who you're handing the AR follow-up to, who posted the meeting recap, who to ask about the pipeline data. A named teammate is something you collaborate with. A faceless tool is something you operate.

**It works with your whole team.** A digital coworker isn't tied to one person. It drafts follow-ups for several account owners, posts action items to a shared channel, and chases missing fields from whichever rep owns the deal. It holds shared context about your business instead of being one person's private assistant. That's the difference between a colleague the team relies on and a tool one person happens to use.

## [2. What a digital coworker can do](#2-what-a-digital-coworker-can-do)

A digital coworker takes over the repeatable, system-heavy work inside a function — the work that's easy to deprioritize and expensive to drop. Here's what that looks like in production, by role.

**Sales development.** An [AI SDR coworker](/use-cases/ai-sdr) researches target accounts, drafts personalized outreach, and keeps the CRM clean — working **3x more targeted accounts per campaign cycle** than a rep doing the research and writing by hand, then handing warm replies to a human.

**Inbound sales.** A [lead response coworker](/use-cases/lead-response) watches your inbound channels and replies in **minutes, not hours**, qualifies the lead, and coordinates scheduling — so qualified demand stops dying in a queue.

**Revenue operations.** A [CRM hygiene coworker](/use-cases/crm-hygiene) logs calls, creates records from email activity, and chases missing fields before pipeline reviews, saving each rep about **three hours a week** of manual data entry.

**Finance.** An [accounts receivable coworker](/use-cases/accounts-receivable) watches the aging report, sends tactful collection emails on cadence, and escalates the hard cases — cutting **days sales outstanding by 50% or more**, which returns weeks of working capital without changing pricing. A [monthly book close coworker](/use-cases/monthly-book-close) categorizes expenses, runs accrual adjustments, and reconciles accounts, cutting roughly **five hours** from each month's manual close.

**Account management.** An [account follow-up coworker](/use-cases/account-follow-up) drafts post-call recaps, updates account records, and tracks renewal commitments so **100% of post-call tasks** stay attached to an owner instead of slipping.

**Team operations.** A [meeting accountability coworker](/use-cases/meeting-accountability) reads internal call transcripts, posts the action items to Slack, and follows up with each owner until the work is actually done — so commitments stop dying the moment people leave the room.

The thread through all of these: the coworker does the operational follow-through, and a human stays in the loop for the exceptions and the judgment calls.

## [3. The organizational impact: more capacity, not more headcount](#3-the-organizational-impact-more-capacity-not-more-headcount)

Here's what changes when a team hires a digital coworker. The repeatable work that used to consume your people — the CRM updates, the invoice chasing, the follow-up drafting, the month-end close — gets absorbed. Your existing team gets that time back.

Look at what that adds up to. Three hours per rep per week on CRM hygiene. Five hours a month on the books. A 50% cut in collection lag. First responses to inbound in minutes instead of hours. None of those required a new hire. They came from moving repeatable work off your people and onto a coworker that runs it reliably in the background.

This is leverage in the literal sense: the same team takes on more without growing. Two things drive it.

**One coworker serves the whole team.** Because a digital coworker isn't tied to one person, the capacity it adds is shared. A single coworker can keep the CRM clean for every rep, chase invoices across the whole aging report, and post action items for every meeting. You're not buying one person an assistant. You're adding capacity the entire team draws on.

**One coworker spans functions.** A digital coworker built on real system access and documented procedures isn't limited to a single narrow task. The same coworker that drafts post-call follow-ups can also track renewal risk and update the account record. As you trust it with more, its surface area grows — the way a good hire takes on more over their first year.

The result is a team that operates above its headcount. The expensive, repeatable work runs in the background. Your people spend their hours on the relationships, decisions, and strategy that actually need a human. You expand what the team can take on — and protect its margin — without expanding the org chart.

That's what Velanir builds: digital coworkers we hire, configure, and operate inside your existing tools, so the coworker walks in already knowing your business and ready to do the work.

## FAQ

A digital coworker is an AI employee that works inside your company's tools the way a remote hire would. It has authenticated access to your CRM, inbox, billing system, and Slack, takes action on real work, and you talk to it in plain language like you would a teammate. It has a name and a picture, works with multiple people across your team, and is managed and given feedback rather than configured. The defining difference from a chatbot is that it completes work in your systems of record instead of only producing text.

A chatbot produces text. A digital coworker produces finished work. A chatbot can tell you how to update your CRM; a digital coworker logs into the CRM and updates it, then drafts the follow-up email, flags the at-risk account in Slack, and reminds the owner two days later. The test is whether the system can change the state of your systems of record. If it can only output recommendations, it's a chatbot. If it can complete the task end to end, it's a coworker.

Because of how you actually work with it. A tool is something one person opens, uses, and closes. A digital coworker drops into your shared workspace, holds real logins to your systems, and works with several people on your team at once. You give it a name and a face, talk to it in Slack like a colleague, and manage it with feedback instead of settings. It behaves like a remote hire, so the coworker framing describes the working relationship more accurately than 'tool' or 'assistant' does.

No. A digital coworker takes the repeatable, system-heavy work off your team's plate — chasing invoices, updating the CRM, drafting follow-ups, closing the books — so your people spend more time on judgment, relationships, and strategy. It expands what your existing team can take on without adding headcount. Humans still set priorities, handle exceptions, and own the decisions that need real judgment. The point is to grow your team's capacity, not to remove the people on it.

The systems where your work actually lives: CRMs like HubSpot and Salesforce, inboxes like Gmail, Slack, billing tools like Stripe and QuickBooks, calendars, meeting transcript tools, and spreadsheets. Access is authenticated and scoped — you decide which systems it touches, which actions need approval first, and which records are off limits. That permission design happens during onboarding, the same way you'd decide what a new hire can and can't touch on their first week.

A digital coworker is not tied to one person. It works with multiple people across a team at once — drafting follow-ups for several account owners, posting meeting action items to a shared Slack channel, and chasing missing CRM fields from whichever rep owns the deal. It holds shared context about your business rather than being one person's private assistant, which is exactly what makes the coworker framing fit better than a personal tool.

Real operational work across functions. A sales coworker researches accounts and drafts personalized outreach for 3x more targeted accounts per cycle. A finance coworker chases overdue invoices and cuts days sales outstanding by 50% or more. A RevOps coworker saves each rep about three hours a week on CRM hygiene. A bookkeeping coworker cuts roughly five hours of manual work from the monthly close. The pattern is the same: it handles the repeatable work inside your tools and brings in a human for the exceptions.

The Digital Coworker Company. Built for all type of operations.

# Senior Digital Marketing Manager

Share on:

**Job Description**

Are you energized to deliver impactful marketing to help ignite love for LEGO in the hearts of kids and adults?

Join us on an exciting journey to accelerate business growth via recruiting new consumers and shoppers into the LEGO brand across India & Emerging Asia!

**Core Responsibilities**

Lead the digital marketing agenda, providing strategic input for campaign development, and leading digital-first projects, that drive significant engagement across priority India & Emerging Asia markets. More specifically, you will:

Drive Active Consumer Base by establishing LEGO play value proposition and driving desire amongst consumers and shoppers

Lead & influence a deep integration of the Digital agenda in marketing plans and activities by using the OESP (Owned, Earned, Shared, Paid) communication model to improve brand awareness and engagement

Create highly effective and sufficient media campaigns by using media expertise, local digital trends, and discussions with media agency of record (Publicis)

Help enhance LEGO brand presence on owned digital platforms (LEGO.com, YouTube, Instagram, SEM)

Manage the LEGO Group's social media account and content calendar in India and drive meaningfulness by creating content for local occasions and topical moments

Provide guidance to retail partners in the development of impactful digital marketing campaigns linked to the aligned brand activation calendar and key insights

Collaborate strongly with multiple partners including internal Marketing sub-teams, Sales teams, and central Marketing teams at the LEGO group

Learn by tracking and analyzing digital marketing performance versus plan via After Action Reviews, benchmarking executions from other business units & other brands

Lead relationships with external agency partners

## 

## **Play your part in our team succeeding!**

This role is reporting to the BU Head of Marketing, sits in India & Emerging Asia business unit (IEA BU), covering 5 key markets across the region. India is a key focus market and this is where majority of this role will be focused.

## 

## **Do you have what it takes?**

Minimum 10 years' experience in branding and digital marketing, preferably in branded goods industry in categories such as: Fashion/ Novelty Retail, Luxury, FMCG, Sports, Snacks/Beverages

Strong strategic, digital and integrated end to end thinking – ability of translating global/regional strategy into local priorities for Digital Marketing with a commercial approach

Drive for results – championing effectiveness and data centricity, bravery to test hypothesis, live a constantly learning mentality, strong analytical and evaluation skills and ability to turn findings into action

Future thinking – be aware of trends and bring outside in perspective, translating them into test & learn pilots with local partners

Strong collaborator and able to work across various internal functions and external partners, in particular with sales teams

Strong ownership & Can-do attitude

Excellent communication and presentation skills with analytical mind

Strong project management skills

Applications are reviewed on an ongoing basis. However, please note we do amend or withdraw our jobs and reserve the right to do so at any time, including prior to any advertised closing date. So, if you're interested in this role we encourage you to apply as soon as possible.

**What’s in it for you?**

Here is what you can expect:

**Family Care Leave** - We offer enhanced paid leave options for those important times.

**Insurances** – All colleagues are covered by our life and disability insurance which provides protection and peace of mind.

**Wellbeing** - We want our people to feel well and thrive. We offer resources and benefits to nurture physical and mental wellbeing along with opportunities to build community and inspire creativity.

**Colleague Discount** – We know you'll love to build, so from day 1 you will qualify for our generous colleague discount.

**Bonus** - We do our best work to succeed together. When goals are reached and if eligible, you'll be rewarded through our bonus scheme.

**Workplace** - When you join the team you'll be assigned a primary workplace location i.e. one of our Offices, stores or factories. Our hybrid work policy means an average of 3 days per week in the office. The hiring team will discuss the policy and role eligibility with you during the recruitment process.

Children are our role models. Their curiosity, creativity and imagination inspire everything we do. We strive to create a diverse, dynamic and inclusive culture of play at the LEGO Group, where everyone feels safe, valued and they belong.

The LEGO Group is highly committed to equal employment opportunity and equal pay and seeks to encourage applicants from all backgrounds (eg. sex, gender identity or expression, race/ethnicity, national origin, sexual orientation, disability, age and religion) to apply for roles in our team.

The LEGO Group is fully committed to Children’s Rights and Child Wellbeing across the globe. Candidates offered positions with high engagement with children are required to take part in Child Safeguarding Background Screening, as a condition of the offer.

Thank you for sharing our global commitment to Children’s Rights.

Just imagine building your dream career.

Then make it real.

**Join the LEGO® team today.**

Share on:

## Find out what is going on Behind the Bricks...

![Father and son playing with LEGO bricks](https://www.lego.com/cdn/cs/careers/assets/blt016d0c197a9beff3/Why_Join_Us.png?width=30&quality=50&crop=16:9)

### Why join us? 🤔

Children inspire our work. They inspire our culture, too. We encourage curiosity, creativity and imagination so everyone can play their part. This creates a safe space for our people to live their purpose and succeed and grow together. It’s all brought to life with play.

![Three LEGO colleagues standing chatting](https://www.lego.com/cdn/cs/careers/assets/bltcea964bd919f6184/Hybrid_Working.png?width=30&quality=50&crop=16:9)

### Workplace Experience & Hybrid Working 💫

Just imagine being empowered to do your best work - every day. Our workplaces are designed to support how you work best, whether that’s at LEGO® Campus, in a hub, store, manufacturing site, office, or at home. With flexible, inspiring spaces and a hybrid approach, we help everyone do their best work, their way.

![Two LEGO colleagues playing with LEGO Bricks](https://www.lego.com/cdn/cs/careers/assets/bltd6a5e9f36d39a174/Our_Leadership_Culture.png?width=30&quality=50&crop=16:9)

### A culture that energises everybody every day. 🤸

Our Leadership Culture is driven by our Leadership Playground, which helps us develop the leadership behaviours we need in the LEGO Group now and for the future.

![LEGO colleague smiling for a photograph](https://www.lego.com/cdn/cs/careers/assets/blte3b9b60b0df2a311/047792_Just_Imagine_-_LISSA_11_1280_x_720.jpg?width=30&quality=50&crop=16:9)

### A red-carpet welcome to a playful workplace. 🤩 Meet Lissa.

As one of our Talent Acquisition Managers, **Lissa** is driven by her passion for people and belief in the power of play. With over five years' experience with us, she plays a crucial role in welcoming new colleagues to the business collaborative work environment.

![LEGO Colleague holding an 'everything is awesome' sign](https://www.lego.com/cdn/cs/careers/assets/blta6facf0dcc0f0847/Untitled_design_-_2026-02-12T112430.789.png?width=30&quality=50&crop=16:9)

### A culture that listens. A role that grows. 🌱 Meet JS.

**JS** began his career as a Finance Business Partner in South Korea before moving into global roles in Denmark, supporting areas such as commercial finance, the commercial development team, and leading global planning. Over time, a growing focus on building capability and shaping the future steered his career in a new direction.
